title: Port gets blocked in rootfull bridge mode after container errors
body:
### Issue Description

Sometimes a rootfull, bridge port will be rendered unusable by containers until I reboot the computer. 

For example, the container with a `8000:8000` port mapping will have a successful `podman exec container_name wget localhost:8000` from within the container, but fail from the host `wget localhost:8000` with:
```
root@sensitive:~# wget localhost:8000
Prepended http:// to 'localhost:8000'
--2025-04-19 14:02:31--  http://localhost:8000/
Resolving localhost (localhost)... ::1, 127.0.0.1
Connecting to localhost (localhost)|::1|:8000... failed: Connection refused.
Connecting to localhost (localhost)|127.0.0.1|:8000... 
```

Restarting the container with a different port binding `8001:8000` gives me back full connectivity to the container.

Running network inspection tools, such as `netstat -tulpn | grep 8000` on the host show that the port is being served by the container when bound to that port, and empty once that container is stopped (or restarted on a new port)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue (unreliable)
1. Run a few containers with quadlets
   -  ex.: containerA ports 8000:8000
3. Start a new container, on a new port, but sharing the same ContainerName as a previous one. 
   - ex.: containerB (named containerA) ports 4444:4444
   - it replaces the other container, systemd thinks both services are running?
4. Name containerB approriately again and restart the systemd service
5. Restart ContainerA systemd service again

### Describe the results you received

Sometimes port 8000 will now be fully unusable, star, stop, restart any containers using it, it will never connect until a computer reboot

### Describe the results you expected

For the port to simply behave normally

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Running inside a debian trixie LXC on Proxmox.

### Additional information

I'm using quadlets, so everything is started through systemctl and I have only seen this when I"m having problems setting up new containers, having to start, restart them a few times and even having ContainerName collision when I'm doing a bad job writing up my container files.

comments.body:
> Running network inspection tools, such as netstat -tulpn | grep 8000 on the host show that the port is being served by the container when bound to that port, and empty once that container is stopped (or restarted on a new port)

That really doesn't matter much, port forwarding as root is done via the firewall.

So you need to check what firewall driver is used (running with `--log-level debug` should show it) and then check your firewall rules, i.e. either iptables or nftables depending on what driver is used. Most likely some rules have no been cleaned up correctly. IF that is the case please provide an expect reproducer on how the container are created and run.

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
